title: The Borrowed Heart
story:
I carry a 26-year-old's heart in my chest.

In 1996, my heart was failing. I'd been having problems since I was 31, but now it was serious. They told me I needed a transplant. At 67, I was old for a transplant. They weren't sure I'd survive.

Somewhere, a family was losing their son. And in their worst moment, they made the decision to donate his organs. A stranger's gift gave me life.

I'll never meet them. I'll never be able to thank them properly. But I think about them every single day. Every morning I wake up and ask myself: Am I using this gift well? Am I worthy of what they gave?

That changes everything. Every conversation matters. Every day is borrowed time. I don't have patience for small talk or pretense. I need to say what I came to say.

You don't need a transplant to live this way. Just remember: your time is limited. What are you doing with it? What will they say about how you spent your days?
followup_questions: If you knew your time was limited—really knew it—what would you do differently?
What have you been putting off that matters?
What will they say about how you spent your days?
